content.text: Every tool available to an AI agent is a capability, a permission, and a risk vector simultaneously. A tool that can search the web is relatively low-risk; a tool that can initiate wire transfers is not. Tool capability datasheets are the structured documentation that makes this risk differentiation explicit, giving agent developers, reviewers, and compliance teams a consistent reference for what each tool can and cannot do — and what the consequences of misuse or failure are.

attrs.alt: Tool Capability Datasheet Structure with six sections: identity, capabilities, permissions/access, limitations/risks, performance envelope, audit/logging, and governance

content.text: What it is

A tool capability datasheet is a structured document that describes, for each tool available to an AI agent, its intended function, input and output schemas, permissions granted, data categories accessed, performance characteristics, known limitations, audit and logging behaviour, and governance requirements. It is the tool-level complement to the agent system card: while the agent card describes the overall system, the tool datasheet describes each component of the agent's action space in enough detail for independent risk assessment.

In practice, a tool in an agentic AI context is a callable function that the agent can invoke at runtime: a database query tool, a web search tool, an email sending tool, a code execution sandbox, a calendar booking API. Each tool represents a distinct capability boundary. The datasheet for each tool must document not just what the tool does when it works correctly, but what happens when it fails, what data it touches, whether its actions are reversible, and what guardrails exist to prevent misuse.
Why it matters

The risk profile of an agentic AI system is largely determined by its tools, not its underlying model. Two agents using the same model but with different tool sets have fundamentally different risk profiles: one with read-only access to a knowledge base is low-risk; one with write access to a financial system is high-risk. Without tool capability datasheets, it is impossible to systematically assess and compare the risk profiles of different agent configurations, or to determine which agents require additional oversight controls.

Tool datasheets also serve a critical role in agent safety: they are the reference document that developers consult when deciding which tools to include in a new agent configuration. A comprehensive datasheet for a 'send email' tool that clearly documents its permissions, rate limits, blast radius on misconfiguration, and required approval thresholds for use in customer-facing contexts helps developers make informed inclusion decisions rather than adding tools by default because they are available.
Benefits
Risk-differentiated tool governance
— Datasheets enable tools to be classified by risk tier (read-only, write, destructive, external-system-access), allowing tiered approval requirements for inclusion in agent configurations.
Informed agent design decisions
— Developers consulting datasheets before building agent configurations understand the risk implications of each tool they include, reducing inadvertent high-risk configurations.
Audit trail completeness
— When incidents occur, the tool datasheet provides a baseline reference for what the tool was designed to do and what it should have logged, making it easier to investigate deviations.
Compliance mapping

— Datasheets can map each tool's data access to specific regulatory frameworks (GDPR Article 6 lawful basis for processing, PCI DSS scope implications), supporting compliance assessment of agent configurations that use the tool.
Change impact assessment
— When a tool's capabilities or permissions change, comparing the old and new datasheets immediately surfaces which agent configurations are affected and what compliance reviews must be triggered.
Trade-offs and limitations
Documentation overhead per tool

— For tool libraries with hundreds of tools, maintaining individual datasheets is a significant ongoing burden. Lightweight tools may not warrant the full datasheet treatment; a tiered approach based on tool risk level is more sustainable.
Third-party tool opacity

— Tools provided by third-party API vendors may not expose sufficient information to complete all datasheet sections accurately. The datasheet must flag these information gaps explicitly rather than leaving sections blank or guessing.
Version drift

— Tool APIs change, sometimes silently. A datasheet that describes the behaviour of tool v1.2 may be materially inaccurate for v1.3 if the vendor made breaking changes to error handling or data access without announcing them.
Context-dependent risk

— The risk of a given tool depends partly on the context in which it is used by the agent. A 'delete record' tool used by a supervised internal assistant is different from the same tool used by a fully autonomous background process. The datasheet documents the tool's intrinsic risk; agent-level context must be assessed separately.
How it works

Tool capability datasheets are authored by the team responsible for the tool — typically the platform or API team that owns the underlying capability. The authoring process begins with the tool's formal specification (OpenAPI schema, function signature, or equivalent) and adds the governance-relevant sections that the specification alone does not capture: limitations, failure modes, permissions, data categories, and audit logging behaviour.

Datasheets are stored in a tool catalogue — a centralised repository that agent developers query when building new configurations. The catalogue provides a searchable index of all available tools with their datasheets, enabling developers to compare alternative tools and select the one with the appropriate capability and risk level for their use case. The catalogue also maintains version history, so that the datasheet version used when a particular agent configuration was approved can be retrieved for audit purposes.

High-risk tool datasheets (those describing tools with write access, external system access, or irreversible actions) require formal review by security and compliance before the tool can be included in any agent configuration. This review is triggered by a flag in the datasheet's risk classification section. The review outcome is recorded in the datasheet, enabling future agent developers to see the approval status without re-requesting review.
Architecture

The tool catalogue integrates with the agent configuration management system so that every agent's tool selection is linked to the specific datasheet versions that were in force at configuration approval time. This link is essential for both compliance purposes (proving that the tool's capabilities were documented and reviewed before inclusion) and incident investigation (determining whether the tool behaved within its documented parameters at the time of an incident). Change notification webhooks from the catalogue alert agent developers and compliance teams when a datasheet is updated, triggering review of affected agent configurations.
When to use it

A tool capability datasheet should exist for every tool that can be included in an agent configuration. For internal utility tools (date formatting, unit conversion) a lightweight datasheet is sufficient. For tools that access sensitive data, interact with external systems, or can take irreversible actions, a full datasheet with security review is required before the tool enters the catalogue. Organisations without a formal tool catalogue should at minimum document the capabilities and limitations of every tool used in any production agent configuration.
How to implement
Define a standard tool datasheet template with required and optional sections appropriate for different tool risk tiers. High-risk tools require more sections than low-risk utility tools.

Establish a tool risk classification framework: at minimum, classify tools by access type (read-only, write, destructive), data sensitivity (PII, financial, health), and reversibility (reversible vs. irreversible actions).
Require a datasheet before any tool enters the tool catalogue. The datasheet is authored by the tool owner and reviewed by security and compliance for tools above a defined risk tier.
Version-control all datasheets alongside the tool code or API specification. Tag each datasheet version with the tool version it describes.
Link tool catalogue entries to agent configurations in the configuration management system, so that each agent configuration references the specific datasheet versions of all included tools.

Implement change notification: when a datasheet is updated (due to tool capability or permission changes), automatically notify agent developers and compliance teams for all agent configurations that include the affected tool.
Schedule annual reviews of all datasheets, with more frequent reviews for tools provided by third-party vendors who may change their API behaviour without announcement.
Examples

# tool-datasheet-send-email-v1.3.yaml

identity:
  tool_name: send_email
  tool_version: '1.3.0'
  category: communication
  owner_team: platform-infrastructure
  datasheet_version: '2025-02-10'
  risk_tier: HIGH
  security_review_date: '2024-11-15'
  security_review_outcome: approved_with_conditions

capabilities:
  description: >
    Sends an email from the configured sender address to one or more recipients.
    Supports plain text and HTML body; attachment support via pre-signed URL references.
  supported_operations:
    - send_single
    - send_bulk (max 50 recipients per call)
  input_schema:
    to: list[email_address] (required)
    cc: list[email_address] (optional)
    subject: string (required, max 998 chars)
    body: string (required, max 10MB)
    body_format: 'text/plain | text/html'
    attachments: list[presigned_url] (optional, max 5)
  output_schema:
    message_id: string
    accepted_recipients: list[email_address]
    rejected_recipients: list[email_address]
    timestamp: ISO8601
  rate_limits:
    per_agent_per_minute: 20
    per_agent_per_hour: 200
    per_agent_per_day: 1000

permissions:
  data_categories_accessed:
    - recipient email addresses (personal data)
    - email body content (may contain personal data)
  external_systems:
    - SendGrid API (email delivery)
    - internal recipient validation service
  auth_mechanism: service account with scoped API key
  pii_processing: yes
  gdpr_lawful_basis_required: yes

limitations_and_risks:
  known_failure_modes:
    - Bulk send fails silently for individual recipients if mailbox full; check rejected_recipients
    - HTML rendering varies across email clients; no preview capability
    - Rate limit exceeded returns 429; caller must implement backoff
  irreversible_actions: yes  # sent emails cannot be recalled
  blast_radius:
    misconfigured_to_field: 'Emails sent to wrong recipients cannot be recalled. High reputational and GDPR risk.'
    bulk_send_loop: 'Agent loop calling bulk send can rapidly exhaust daily quota and trigger spam filters.'
  required_human_review:
    - Any use in externally-facing customer communications contexts
    - Any send to distribution list > 10 recipients
    - Any send containing financial or health information

performance:
  typical_latency_ms: 800-2500
  p99_latency_ms: 8000
  availability_sla: '99.5%'
  cost_per_call_usd: 0.0001

audit_logging:
  what_is_logged:
    - tool call timestamp
    - agent session ID
    - recipient list (hashed)
    - subject line
    - outcome (success/failure)
    - message ID
  body_logged: false  # body content is not logged due to potential PII
  retention_days: 730  # 2 years
  siem_integrated: true

governance:
  approval_required_to_include: yes  # for agents in customer-facing contexts
  approval_authority: 'Head of Product + DPO'
  change_notification: 'agent-tool-changes@company.com'
  incident_contact: 'platform-security@company.com'

# Tool risk classification helper
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import List
from enum import Enum

class RiskTier(Enum):
    LOW = 'low'
    MEDIUM = 'medium'
    HIGH = 'high'
    CRITICAL = 'critical'

@dataclass
class ToolRiskClassifier:
    tool_name: str
    access_type: str              # 'read' | 'write' | 'delete' | 'execute'
    pii_access: bool
    financial_data_access: bool
    health_data_access: bool
    external_system_access: bool
    actions_reversible: bool
    max_blast_radius: str         # 'local' | 'team' | 'org' | 'external'

    def classify(self) -> RiskTier:
        score = 0

        # Access type scoring
        access_scores = {'read': 0, 'write': 2, 'delete': 3, 'execute': 3}
        score += access_scores.get(self.access_type, 0)

        # Data sensitivity
        if self.pii_access: score += 2
        if self.financial_data_access: score += 2
        if self.health_data_access: score += 3

        # External system access
        if self.external_system_access: score += 2

        # Irreversibility
        if not self.actions_reversible: score += 2

        # Blast radius
        blast_scores = {'local': 0, 'team': 1, 'org': 2, 'external': 3}
        score += blast_scores.get(self.max_blast_radius, 0)

        if score <= 2:
            return RiskTier.LOW
        elif score <= 5:
            return RiskTier.MEDIUM
        elif score <= 9:
            return RiskTier.HIGH
        else:
            return RiskTier.CRITICAL

# Examples
email_tool = ToolRiskClassifier(
    tool_name='send_email',
    access_type='write',
    pii_access=True,
    financial_data_access=False,
    health_data_access=False,
    external_system_access=True,
    actions_reversible=False,
    max_blast_radius='external',
)
print(f'send_email: {email_tool.classify().value}')  # HIGH

read_kb = ToolRiskClassifier(
    tool_name='knowledge_base_search',
    access_type='read',
    pii_access=False,
    financial_data_access=False,
    health_data_access=False,
    external_system_access=False,
    actions_reversible=True,
    max_blast_radius='local',
)
print(f'knowledge_base_search: {read_kb.classify().value}')  # LOW
Real-world example

A professional services firm built an internal AI assistant for their M&A due diligence team that included a file system access tool, a document analysis tool, and a database query tool. When a new attorney wanted to add a 'draft and send email' tool to allow the agent to draft communications to counterparties, the tool catalogue required a datasheet review. The security review of the email tool datasheet identified that the tool could send emails to external parties without human review for messages under a certain token length, creating a risk that the agent could inadvertently disclose confidential deal terms to incorrect recipients.

Rather than blocking the tool entirely, the security review resulted in an updated tool datasheet that classified the tool as HIGH risk with a required human review condition: any email send to an external domain required explicit human approval through a review queue before delivery. The agent developer implemented this constraint in the agent's tool execution configuration. The modified datasheet with the review condition was recorded as the approved version for use in M&A contexts. When the same email tool was later proposed for use in an internal-only HR assistant where all recipients were company employees, a separate review determined that external recipient risk did not apply, and the tool was approved at MEDIUM risk for that context.
Common mistakes
Treating tool documentation as developer documentation
— OpenAPI specifications and function signatures are developer documentation; tool datasheets are governance documents that must be readable by compliance and security teams, not just engineers.
Missing irreversibility flag

— Whether a tool's actions can be reversed is one of the most important risk characteristics. Tools that cannot be rolled back (sent emails, executed transfers, deleted records) require fundamentally different oversight than reversible ones.
Not documenting blast radius

— 'Blast radius' — the scope of harm if the tool is misconfigured or misused — is often not documented because it requires adversarial thinking rather than just describing correct behaviour. This omission consistently surprises teams when actual misuse occurs.
No third-party change monitoring

— Tools backed by third-party APIs can change their behaviour, permissions, or data retention practices without announcing it. Datasheets for third-party tools must be reviewed against vendor changelogs regularly.
Best practices
Classify every tool by risk tier before catalogue entry
— Apply the risk classification at intake so that the appropriate review level is automatically triggered.
Document failure modes adversarially
— Write the limitations section by asking 'how could this tool cause harm if an agent used it incorrectly, at the wrong time, or in an unexpected context?' rather than just documenting known bugs.
Link agent configurations to datasheet versions
— Record which specific datasheet version was in force when each agent configuration was approved. This link is needed for both compliance evidence and incident investigation.
Use tiered datasheet templates

— A utility tool that converts temperatures does not need the same documentation depth as a tool that can execute database writes. Tiered templates reduce overhead while ensuring adequate coverage for high-risk tools.
Include the cost per call in the datasheet

— Agents operating in loops can call tools thousands of times; knowing the per-call cost is essential for budget planning and for identifying runaway agent behaviours that generate unexpected tool call volumes.
Performance and scalability

Tool catalogues typically grow to dozens or hundreds of entries as an organisation's agent programme matures. At this scale, the catalogue must be searchable by tool name, risk tier, data category, and external system access to allow developers to quickly find appropriate tools for their use case. The datasheet maintenance burden scales with the number of tools and the frequency of vendor API changes; automated monitoring of third-party API changelogs and direct integration with the tool's version control system can significantly reduce the manual maintenance overhead.

Comparison with alternatives
Approach
Strengths
Weaknesses
Structured tool datasheet (this topic)
Governance-ready; risk-tiered; searchable in catalogue
Maintenance overhead; requires structured process
OpenAPI / JSON Schema spec only
Auto-generated; always up-to-date with implementation
No risk classification, limitations, or governance content
Code comments / inline docs
Zero overhead; always near the code
Not accessible to non-engineers; no catalogue searchability
No tool documentation
No overhead whatsoever
Cannot assess agent risk; unacceptable for regulated contexts
Security and reliability

Tool datasheets for high-risk tools contain information that could be exploited by adversaries: knowledge of which external systems are accessed, what authentication mechanisms are used, what rate limits exist (enabling targeted rate-exhaustion attacks), and what the blast radius of tool misuse is. Access to full datasheets for high-risk tools should be restricted to engineering teams, security, and compliance. Publicly accessible catalogue entries can describe a tool's general purpose and risk tier without disclosing security-sensitive implementation details.
Key takeaways
The risk profile of an agentic AI system is largely determined by its tools; tool capability datasheets are the primary governance mechanism for managing that risk.
Datasheets go beyond API specifications to document governance-relevant characteristics: risk tier, data categories accessed, reversibility, blast radius, audit logging, and required approvals.
A tool catalogue with searchable datasheets enables agent developers to make informed, risk-aware tool selection decisions rather than adding tools by availability.
Irreversibility is one of the most important tool risk characteristics and must be explicitly documented; tools with irreversible actions require stricter oversight.
Third-party tools require monitoring for vendor-side changes that may alter their capabilities or data access without announcement.
Linking agent configurations to specific datasheet versions provides the audit trail needed to demonstrate that tool risks were assessed and accepted before each agent configuration was deployed.
Use tiered datasheet templates: deep documentation for high-risk tools, lightweight coverage for low-risk utilities, to keep the overall maintenance burden proportionate to actual risk.
